options.explanation: Correct. An elif chain stops at the first true condition. score >= 70 is true, so Python prints "C" and skips the rest of the chain entirely — it never even checks the 80 or 90 branches. To grade correctly, order the conditions from the highest threshold down.

This would be right if each condition were checked independently, but an elif chain stops at the FIRST match. score >= 70 matches first, so the 90 branch is never reached. Order matters: put the highest threshold first.

options.text: C, B, and A on three lines
options.explanation: That is what a stack of three independent if statements would do. But elif chains the conditions, so only the first true branch runs and the rest are skipped.

options.text: Nothing — the conditions are out of order so Python raises an error
options.explanation: Out-of-order conditions are a logic bug, not a syntax error. Python runs the chain happily and prints "C". The wrong order produces a wrong answer, not a crash.
Correct. or returns the first truthy operand. 0 is falsy, so or moves on to "hello", which is truthy, and returns it. Note it returns the operand itself, not True.
A common assumption, but or does not strictly return a boolean. It returns one of the operands — here the first truthy one, "hello". (It happens to be truthy, but the value itself is the string.)
or returns the first TRUTHY operand, not the first one. 0 is falsy, so or skips it and returns "hello". (0 would be the answer for 0 and "hello", since and returns the first falsy operand.)
or only evaluates to a falsy result if every operand is falsy. Here "hello" is truthy, so the expression is "hello" — and even an all-falsy or returns the last operand, not necessarily False.

questions.question: Why does the guard `if text and text[0].isdigit():` avoid a crash when text is the empty string?

options.text: and short-circuits: text is falsy, so Python never evaluates text[0]
options.explanation: Correct. The empty string is falsy. For and, once the left side is false the whole expression is false, so Python stops and never runs text[0].isdigit() — which would otherwise raise an IndexError on an empty string.

options.text: isdigit() returns False for an empty string, so the condition is just False
options.explanation: The problem happens before isdigit() is ever called: text[0] on an empty string raises IndexError. The short-circuit is what prevents text[0] from running at all.

options.text: Python automatically replaces text[0] with an empty string when text is empty
options.explanation: Python does no such substitution. text[0] on an empty string is a real IndexError. The guard works only because and short-circuits and skips the indexing entirely.

options.text: and evaluates both sides, but ignores the error on the right
options.explanation: and does not swallow errors. It avoids the error by not evaluating the right side at all — once the left operand is falsy, the result is known and the right side is skipped.
